notable.notable_blurb: Founded Female Auxiliary of the American Colonization Society

narrative_blocks.detail: Blackford kept a journal, Notes Illustrative of the Wrongs of Slavery, recording her evolving beliefs on the subject. She opposed abolition itself as 'impracticable' while remaining personally opposed to slavery — a position that defined the colonizationist movement. Her brother was a missionary in Liberia 1837-1843. She lived to 93, and in 1892 her six surviving children gathered for the first time since the war to celebrate her 90th birthday together in Alexandria. Her grandson Launcelot Minor Blackford wrote her biography, Mine Eyes Have Seen The Glory (Harvard University Press, 1954). She was the sister of Dr. James Monroe Minor (X00513), who married Ellen Josephine Pierrepont (HD1598), a 6th-generation Hooker descendant. (Wikipedia)
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Berkeley Minor Blackford founded the Fredericksburg and Falmouth Female Auxiliary of the American Colonization Society in 1829, taught enslaved people to read the Bible in defiance of Virginia law, and maintained decades of correspondence with anti-slavery activists. Yet when the Civil War came, five of her eight children served in the Confederate Army. 'To see my sons arrayed against one part of their country,' she wrote in 1861, 'is a sorrow that makes me feel the grave is the only place for me.'

narrative_blocks.header: Five sons fought for the Confederacy
narrative_blocks.body: The woman who founded Fredericksburg’s anti-slavery auxiliary at twenty-six lived to watch five of her sons fight for the Confederacy. She survived the war by thirty years, dying at ninety-three.

Yet when the Civil War came, five of her eight children served in the Confederate Army. narrative_blocks.header: Five sons fought for the Confederacy
narrative_blocks.body: The woman who founded Fredericksburg’s anti-slavery auxiliary at twenty-six lived to watch five of her sons fight for the Confederacy. She survived the war by thirty years, dying at ninety-three.

narrative_blocks.header: He filed Virginia's first emancipation bill
narrative_blocks.body: In 1782 Minor introduced a bill in the Virginia legislature for the gradual emancipation of the enslaved, the first such measure in the state. He raised the cause more than once, well ahead of his planter contemporaries.

Trained in law under George Wythe at William and Mary, Minor served as commonwealth's attorney at Fredericksburg and sat in the House of Delegates. He was commissioned a brigadier general during the War of 1812.

narrative_blocks.header: He died mid-speech before the Electoral College
narrative_blocks.body: Minor was struck down by apoplexy in 1816 while addressing the Electoral College at Richmond. His daughter Mary Berkeley Minor Blackford carried his antislavery convictions into the next generation.
